title: Marbled bundt cake
subtitle: Vanilla and chocolate sponge swirled together in a bundt tin
excerpt: A large, celebratory bundt with a soft crumb and a dramatic cross-section of interlocked vanilla and chocolate swirls. The batter is made once, split into two portions, with cocoa stirred into one half. The two batters are added to the tin in alternating scoops, then a skewer is drawn through in a zigzag to create the marble pattern. Simple to make and keeps well.
sourceNotes: Marble cake (Marmorkuchen) is a German tradition dating to the nineteenth century, combining a Rührkuchen (beaten butter cake) base with a chocolate stripe. The technique of splitting a single batter and swirling the two portions together is documented in German home-baking books from the 1880s onwards. The bundt tin adaptation became common in mid-twentieth-century American baking after Nordic Ware popularised the tin in the 1950s. The recipe here synthesises the German marble cake tradition with the bundt format.

content.text: The key to a bold marble is light
. Two or three passes of the skewer are enough. Grease the bundt tin very thoroughly, reaching into every groove, then flour it; bundt cakes are notorious for sticking.

Heat the oven to 170°C conventional (150°C fan). Butter and flour the bundt tin very thoroughly, tapping out the excess. Beat the butter and sugar until pale and fluffy, about 5 minutes. Beat in the eggs one at a time. Beat in the soured cream and vanilla. Fold in the flour, baking powder, and salt until just combined.
Divide the batter equally into two bowls. Stir the sifted cocoa and the milk into one bowl until evenly combined. The chocolate batter will be slightly thicker than the vanilla.

Alternate large spoonfuls of vanilla and chocolate batter into the tin, layering them rather than mixing. Draw a skewer or knife through the batter in a loose zigzag two or three times. Smooth the top. Bake for 50 to 60 minutes until a skewer inserted at the thickest point comes out clean and the cake is starting to pull away from the sides. Leave in the tin for 15 minutes, then invert onto a rack. Cool completely before dusting with icing sugar or drizzling with a simple chocolate glaze.
